titleStmt.title: The Ambassador in Korea (Muccio) to the Department of State

head.hi: The Ambassador in Korea (Muccio) to the Department of State

div.p: 1350. Rptd info CINCUNC (personal for Gen Clark), EUSAK Advance (personal for Gen Van Fleet) as DIP 320 by other means. Re Deptel 941, June 10.In this telegram, the Department of State instructed Muccio to inform UNCURK, Rhee, and the leaders of the National Assembly that the U.S. Government supported the UNCURK letter of June 7, which reiterated its request to lift martial law and to release those Assemblymen under arrest, and which called for a “political holiday” of ten days during which efforts to reach an agreement between the ROK executive and legislature could be made (795B.00/6–952). A text of the UNCURK letter is in UN document A/2187, pp. 16–17.
I have just spent the most futile hour with Pres Rhee I have ever spent with him.

Upon arrival by appointment at my request, I mentioned my concern over the posters throughout the city damning foreigners for interfering in Korean affairs. Pres Rhee said he would not have Koreans take such a stand against “the hand that was feeding”. He immediately called the chief of his palace guard, Capt Kim and spoke heatedly to him in Korean. The Pres then went on to say that he understood special police precautions had been placed about foreign installations and residences. (I had expressed surprise of need for this to Lee Bum-Suk and to Paik Ta-Chin previous day). He said that was very repugnant to Min and asked me to notify him personally at once of any such acts so that he might correct them.

I then conveyed to him the first para of ref tel re US support of UNCURK’s ltr of June 7. This touched him off on one of the most irrational explosions I have ever had to endure:—He said Pres Truman and the Amer Govt had been too drastic and did not understand his noble purposes, went into heated detail about UNCURK’s interference and leakages to press, claimed he was alone, cld not depend on anyone, world was against him, he was tired, hoped successor wld do better, needed a rest and then most fervently described the plot to take over govt and make a deal with the Communists.
The Pres was so completely irrational, that it is impossible to make detailed summary of his wanderings.

I left with impression he was both physically and mentally exhausted and did not know to whom to turn. He made vague references to turning things over to someone or other and seeing whether they cld do better.

In the six days since my return, I have been forced to the conclusion that we cannot expect effective leadership from Pres Rhee. There is no question in my mind that he is absolutely convinced of a plot against him and Korea. I feel strongly that the “Lee Bum-Suk” group has practically taken Rhee over completely. Our main immediate problem is to thwart the Lee Bum-Suk group from taking over. This group is now finalizing its control over the police—it has the martial law Commander Gen Won in its camp—and has made certain moves towards the Korean army, thus far unsuccessfully in the latter field.

I am keeping in very close touch with the chairman of UNCURK and Gen Van Fleet. I am still doing everything possible to impress upon all Koreans I know the need for some sort of satisfactory solution to the present crucial situation. This is merely a prelim report to forewarn of the probability of a precipitous deterioration of the situation here which may even be in the form of a violent forceful move to eliminate all opposition at any time, by Lee Bum-Suk acting ostensibly in the name of Rhee.

Attempts at clarification of four questions posed by the DeptIn telegram 941 to Pusan, June 10, the Department of State asked Muccio four questions concerning the internal situation in Korea: 1) Was it certain Rhee would accept and not oppose the National Assembly’s choice for President other than himself if his amendment for direct election was first passed; 2) or would he insist on direct election and declare null and void the Assembly election of his successor; 3) did Rhee envision direct election in 1956 or before; and 4) what was the status of a compromise amendment proposed by the ROK Prime Minister (reported in telegram 1259 from Pusan, June 3, p. 293) (795B.00/6–952). will be made in separate message.
